label: 122_brexit_equivalence_financial centre_uk
label_human: Brexit impact on UK financial centre
description: The UK's departure from the EU has raised questions about London's status as a global financial hub. Discussions centered on passporting rights, equivalence frameworks, and the role of central clearing counterparties in maintaining market access for UK firms.

earliest_articles.title: Brexit: The potential for a financial catastrophe and long-term consequences for the UK financial sector
earliest_articles.excerpt: On 23 June 2016, there will be a referendum to decide whether the UK should remain in or leave the EU. Proponents on both sides of the Brexit debate argue that the outcome will have important and long-lasting consequences. The latest Centre for Macroeconomics (CFM) survey focuses on the consequences for the stability…

earliest_articles.title: The sun still rises: London after Brexit
earliest_articles.excerpt: London’s position as global city will not necessarily be substantially threatened by the United Kingdom’s vote to leave the European Union, but it will require some very important adjustments and confident negotiations, starting right now. Since 1990, London’s global city status has evolved rapidly through its various…

earliest_articles.title: On the financial market consequences of Brexit
earliest_articles.excerpt: The UK vote yesterday to leave the European Union will have widespread consequences for financial markets, creating both opportunities and problems. Brexit may increase global financial stability since heterogeneous financial markets and economic systems increase financial stability (as was discussed here on Vox in…

newest_articles.title: Reboot of the UK-EU relationship in financial services regulation
newest_articles.excerpt: Brexit continues to pose unique challenges for financial sector policymakers in the EU, as the most important financial centre in Europe is now outside its regulatory framework (Macrae et al. 2016, Jackson, 2016). The UK, on the other hand, considers its financial sector a potential growth engine at the global level…

newest_articles.title: London as a financial centre since Brexit: evidence from the 2022 BIS Triennial Survey
newest_articles.excerpt: London as a financial centre since Brexit: evidence from the 2022 BIS Triennial Survey BIS Bulletin | No 65 | 16 December 2022 Key takeaways - Historically, London has enjoyed an outsized role in euro-denominated financial transactions. We examine the latest evidence on whether Brexit has affected its central role in…

newest_articles.title: After orderly Brexit, a new European financial landscape starts to emerge
newest_articles.excerpt: The United Kingdom’s exit from the European single market on January 1 has sent trade in goods plummeting amid much confusion. By contrast, Brexit was carried out in an orderly manner in the financial sector, despite significant movement of trading in shares and derivatives away from the City of London. After five…
